_meta.title: Niki Weiss content library — Q&A, action items, scenario-based education
_meta.purpose: Grounding data for the low-token AI agent: deterministic routing/rules only, no engineering/DDB/worksheet-validation content included by design (scoped to Niki's clinical/educational logic per 2026-07-01 direction).
_meta.source: Extracted from niki_routing_rules_v1.html (6 Otter session transcripts: Peter Chmiel Sprint 1+2, Jayne Lammers, Ellen Feinberg, Sue, Billie), cross-checked against Elisa Seeherman meet-and-greet transcript for tone/glossary.
_meta.status: DRAFT — content and priority ordering not yet confirmed by Niki. Use as working content-library seed; do not present as clinically final. Domain tags (added 2026-07-02) on actionItems and the definitionsGlossary array (added 2026-07-02) are engineering-authored classifications/definitions derived from this content for UI organization — not independently reviewed by Niki. Treat as a working scaffold, not clinically final; each glossary entry carries its own 'validated': false flag for tracking. Digital assessment questions D1-D3 (added 2026-07-02) are drafted in Niki's voice from client-provided source questions, converted to fit this app's format -- captured as MemberContext flags (noLegacyContact, noSocialMediaPlan, noPasswordManager) but NOT YET wired into lead-profile routing or action items, since no digital-gap action items have been authored yet. Unvalidated by Niki. profile_digital_gap (added 2026-07-02) is drafted, unvalidated content -- practical digital-access how-tos matching Niki's established voice/format, written to answer the digital-assessment gap (D1-D3). Always parallel/additive like profile_business_owner, never a lead profile. Each actionItem carries a `flag` field -- rules_engine.build_plan() includes an item only if that specific MemberContext flag is true, not the whole profile at once. profile_health_gap and contextQuestions H1-H3 (added 2026-07-03) follow the exact same pattern as profile_digital_gap/D1-D3 -- drafted, unvalidated, not yet reviewed by Niki. generalResources (added 2026-07-03) is ORIGINAL general-knowledge content written to broaden what the chat agent can helpfully answer beyond the matched plan (e.g. 'how do I write a will') -- it is NOT sourced from any specific external website, and like everything else in this file, unvalidated by Niki -- treat as a starting draft, not legal/financial guidance to rely on as final.

coreFraming.philosophy: Break through the taboo of talking about death — but the empowerment is 'live fully, die ready.' Death is not a topic, it's an administrative and emotional reality that needs a plan.
keyLines: The worst time to plan a funeral is when someone's dead.
Debt is not dead anymore — there's a digital piece too.
It's not the sandwich generation anymore, it's the Panini generation — so squished in.
Acknowledge it: death is taboo, people avoid it, but not planning will cost time, money, and emotional stress when an event occurs.
How do we make it a roadmap rather than a treasure hunt?

threeScenarios.name: You die tomorrow
threeScenarios.focus: Your people taking action — they need documents, access, and a roadmap.
coreDocuments: Power of attorney — healthcare / medical advance directive
threeScenarios.quote: If you die tomorrow, there are three documents you absolutely need: a will, power of attorney, medical advance directive.

threeScenarios.name: You get a diagnosis
threeScenarios.focus: You're still alive but not able to make your own decisions — someone empowered to act on your known wishes.
elements: Healthcare proxy knows your wishes in detail
Trusted person empowered while you're incapacitated
threeScenarios.quote: Durable power of attorney for financial, durable power of attorney for healthcare — you're still alive, but you're not of sound mind, not making good decisions.

threeScenarios.name: Long-term care
threeScenarios.focus: Sustained care needs, usually last unless there's an active health urgency.
elements: Medicaid planning if assets are limited
threeScenarios.quote: If she needs to go into a facility, she doesn't have enough assets for that, so I'm going to work the Medicaid angle as she starts spinning down.

contextQuestions.question: Tell me about your family situation. Do you have a partner? Children — how old? Are your parents living? Do you have siblings?
contextQuestions.purpose: This single question determines the entire routing priority — which situation profile(s) apply.

contextQuestions.question: Is your parent living independently? Do they have the three core documents — will, power of attorney, and medical directive?
contextQuestions.purpose: If parent has no documents, parent planning gets prioritized above the member's own.

contextQuestions.question: What do you currently have in place? Will? Power of attorney? Medical directive? Have those been updated recently?
contextQuestions.purpose: Determines whether to start from scratch (clean slate) or pressure-test an existing plan.

contextQuestions.question: Have you had any major life changes in the last 5 years — divorce, marriage, new child, death of a named person, major asset change?
contextQuestions.purpose: If a post-divorce/major-change flag is set, beneficiary audit becomes first priority regardless of everything else.

contextQuestions.question: Are you currently the primary caregiver for someone else? Do you own or co-own a business?
contextQuestions.purpose: Each adds a separate parallel checklist to the member's plan.

contextQuestions.question: What's the best way for you to track this? Notebook, spreadsheet, Google Doc, app? How do we keep you accountable?
contextQuestions.purpose: Determines how action items should be delivered/tracked back to the member.

contextQuestions.question: Do you have a healthcare proxy or medical power of attorney -- someone who can make medical decisions for you if you can't?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether the member has any healthcare decision-maker named at all.

options.label: Named, but we haven't made it official

contextQuestions.question: Do you have an advance directive or living will that spells out your care wishes, like a DNR?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether care wishes are documented in writing, not just known informally.

contextQuestions.question: If you were in a serious accident tomorrow, does your healthcare proxy actually know your wishes in detail -- not just that they're named?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags the gap between 'someone is named' and 'that person actually knows what you want' -- Niki's own distinction, see peopleBeforeDocuments.

options.label: They're named but we haven't really discussed it

contextQuestions.question: If you died tomorrow and your phone went with you, is there a Legacy Contact set up on your account so someone could actually get in?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether the member has any digital-access plan at all for their phone/accounts.

contextQuestions.question: Have you named someone to handle your social media if you couldn't do it yourself — and do they actually know what to do?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether social media has a named person AND real instructions, not just a name.

options.label: Yes — named, and they have instructions

contextQuestions.question: Are your passwords and logins somewhere a trusted person could actually find and use — a password manager, anything real?
contextQuestions.purpose: Flags whether login/credential access exists anywhere outside the member's own head.

peopleBeforeDocuments.principle: Niki never starts with 'you need a will.' She starts with 'who are the people in your life?' — abstract legal concepts become concrete by attaching real names first, then mapping people to document roles.
peopleBeforeDocuments.quote: Those people you identify as emergency contacts — the question is, are they going to be in your will? Are they going to be the executor, power of attorney, medical advance directive?

mapping.question: Who would you call if something happened to you tonight? Does that person know they're your first call?
If you died, who takes the first action? That person is probably your executor.
Who do you trust most with your financial decisions if you can't make them?
Who knows your healthcare wishes? Who would you trust to make those decisions?
If something happened to both you and your spouse, who takes the kids? Does that person know?
If you were gone tomorrow, who runs the business? Do they have what they need?
Does the neighbor/caregiver have your phone number? Could they reach you if something happened?

situationProfiles.name: Aging parent with limited documentation
questions: Does your parent have a will, power of attorney (financial + healthcare), and medical advance directive?
Do YOU have copies of those documents?
Do you know if they have a DNR? Where is it physically kept?
Does whoever would respond in an emergency (neighbor, building super, aide) have YOUR contact information?
Do you have a key to their home? Do you know the security code?
Is there a safe deposit box? Are you authorized on it?
Are you and your sibling(s) both named as co-executors or co-POA holders?
When was the will last updated? Does it reflect current wishes?
priorityOrder: Parent documents BEFORE member's own — get will, POA financial, POA healthcare, medical directive from parent's lawyer
Confirm DNR status and physical location
Emergency access — neighbor's number, house key, security code
Safe deposit box — are you authorized?
Member's own three documents (deferred until parent situation is addressed)

actionItems.text: Ask your parent for copies of their will, power of attorney, and medical advance directive.
actionItems.script: Mom, I'm doing my estate planning. I realized I don't have a copy of yours. If something happened to you tomorrow, I would be responsible. Can you give me a copy of your will, power of attorney, and medical advance directive?

actionItems.text: Ask: Do you have a DNR? Where is it kept? Check likely spots — near front door, refrigerator, hospital copy.
Get the neighbor's / building manager's contact info. Give them YOUR number.
Find the house key. Know the security code. Make sure a sibling also has access.
Call the bank: is my name on her safe deposit box? If not, how do we add it?
Ask your parent when their will was last updated and whether it still reflects who they want.

situationProfiles.quote: I just don't want you to get the call and not be able to act.

situationProfiles.name: Young children, no guardian named
questions: If something happened to both you and your spouse/partner tonight, who takes the kids?
Does that person know they would be named? Have they agreed?
Have you and your spouse agreed on the same person?
Does that person have the capacity — financially, physically, emotionally?
Is there life insurance adequate to fund raising your children to adulthood?
Does your spouse's family know what your wishes are for the children?
situationProfiles.specialNote: Do NOT name minor children directly as beneficiaries on accounts — assets need a trust or UTMA custodial account to avoid court control until the child reaches adulthood.
priorityOrder: Guardian conversation between spouses — agree on one person (and a backup)
Confirm with the named guardian that they know and agree
Will with guardian named + executor named
Life insurance review — is coverage enough to raise them?
Trust or custodial account consideration (assets shouldn't pass directly to minors)
Emergency contact card for schools, activities, pediatrician

actionItems.text: Have the conversation with your partner: who is the guardian? Agree before next session.
Contact that person and ask directly.
actionItems.script: We'd like to name you as guardian for our children. Are you willing and prepared?

actionItems.text: See an estate attorney: will + guardian named + executor named (same or different person).
Review life insurance: is the amount adequate to cover child-rearing through age 22?
Confirm extended family knows the plan.
actionItems.script: If something happened to us both, who takes the kids? Does your wife's sister know? Have you had that conversation?

situationProfiles.quote: If something happened to us both, who takes the kids? Does your wife's sister know? Have you had that conversation?

situationProfiles.name: Solo ager (no partner, no children)
questions: Who would you call if something happened to you tonight?
Does that person know they're your first call?
Do you have a healthcare proxy who knows your wishes in detail?
Are all your accounts set up with transfer on death (TOD) or payable on death (POD)?
Have you thought about who gets your assets — people or organizations?
Do you have IP, a business, or a website that needs a shutdown plan?
Are you the informal caregiver for a parent with limited assets? Have you thought about Medicaid planning?
priorityOrder: Transfer on death (TOD) on all brokerage accounts + payable on death (POD) on all bank accounts — prevents probate without a natural heir
Healthcare proxy who truly knows your wishes (not just named on paper)
Professional fiduciary consideration for financial decisions if no trusted person
Digital legacy — business shutdown plan, IP documentation, account access
Charitable/organizational beneficiary conversations
Childless trust consideration if assets are significant

actionItems.text: Call each financial institution.
actionItems.script: I'm calling to say I'm doing estate planning. I just want to make sure: do I have transfer on death set up on this account? If not, how do we set that up?

actionItems.text: Sit with your healthcare proxy and walk through your actual wishes, not just 'I trust you to decide.'
Document your digital legacy: the accounts, the business, the clients. Who shuts it down? What are the instructions?
Use an online notary for remaining documents.
Freeze your credit at all three bureaus.
actionItems.quote: If you don't need your credit available right now — not buying a car, not buying a house — your credit should be frozen. It's the best protection against the data breaches happening right now.

situationProfiles.quote: There's actually a niche market — childless trusts. They only deal with people who have no children, but they have assets. They assign fiduciaries, designate organizations, decide on distribution terms.

situationProfiles.name: Business owner / entrepreneur
questions: If something happened to you tomorrow, who runs the business?
Does that person have the credentials, logins, and access they need?
Do you have key person insurance?
Do you have old 401k accounts from previous employers that haven't been rolled over?
Are your IP, patents, or content assets documented and owned clearly?
Are your corporate/founding documents (incorporation, operating agreement) accessible?
situationProfiles.criticalRule: Business plan and personal plan are two separate checklists. Your personal life would be fine. Your business life — that's a different question.
priorityOrder: Name the business successor / 'business executor' — who takes over
Key person insurance review
401k rollover from any previous employers (extremely common gap among founders who left corporate jobs)
Business contact document: vendors, clients, accounts — separate from personal
IP ownership documentation
Corporate documents in a crucial doc box (separate section from personal documents)

actionItems.text: Identify the business successor and brief them.
Research or review key person insurance.
Locate all old 401k accounts from previous employers and initiate rollover.
Create a business contacts document (clients, key vendors, accounts, login instructions) — separate from personal documents.
Add corporate documents to your crucial doc box as a separate section.

situationProfiles.quote: A lot of founders left their corporate jobs and have 401ks that need to be rolled over. They left their ex-spouse on as a beneficiary — oh yeah.

situationProfiles.name: Post-divorce or major life change in last 5 years
situationProfiles.urgency: beneficiary audit is first action before all else
questions: Have you checked ALL beneficiary designations since that change? (Each account separately — they do not update automatically)
401k, IRA, life insurance, brokerage — on each one: who is listed?
Is an ex-spouse still listed anywhere? (More common than people expect)
Has your will been updated since the life change?
Have the people named in your POA and healthcare proxy changed?
priorityOrder: Audit ALL beneficiary designations: 401k, IRA, brokerage (TOD), life insurance, pension
Update will to reflect current wishes and current named persons
Update POA and healthcare proxy if named persons have changed
Review assets acquired since the change: do they have the right designations?

actionItems.text: List every account with a beneficiary designation. Pull the form or call the institution. Verify who is listed. Update immediately if wrong.
Check specifically whether an ex-spouse is still listed anywhere — retirement account beneficiary designations override what the will says.
Schedule time with an estate attorney to update the will.
Check whether the people named in your POA are still the right people.

quotes: They left their ex-spouse on as a beneficiary of their 401k after 20 years. Oh yeah. I didn't even think about that.
She had a wealth manager but she had no will. She died intestate. The adult children are scrambling — they can't access her accounts. It has to go through probate.

situationProfiles.name: Nothing done, clean slate
situationProfiles.approach: No judgment, no urgency panic. You don't have a plan because life gets busy — it's not prioritized, that's why it takes time. Start with ONE concrete physical action. Give the full scope so they can see the project, then sequence it themselves.
questions: Who would be the first person called if something happened to you tonight?
Do you know where your birth certificate is?
If your partner died tomorrow, could you find all the financial accounts?
Is there a fireproof box somewhere with your important documents?
priorityOrder: Emergency contact card — who are the people? (seeds all document decisions)
Asset inventory — what do you have? Update beneficiary column on each account.
Crucial doc box — find a fireproof box, identify what goes in it
Three core documents — will, POA financial, POA healthcare / medical directive
Digital vault setup (deferred until physical is established)

actionItems.text: Print and fill out emergency contact cards.
Find and update your asset inventory spreadsheet. Add a beneficiary column, TOD/POD status, and a point of contact.
Make ONE call to ONE financial institution this week.
actionItems.script: Am I set up with TOD/POD on this account?

actionItems.text: Find your birth certificate (the gateway physical-document exercise).
Have one conversation with your spouse about who would be the guardian for the kids.

situationProfiles.quote: How do we make it a roadmap rather than a treasure hunt?

situationProfiles.name: Has some documents, needs pressure testing
situationProfiles.approach: You've done work, but have you pressure-tested it? Review what exists, identify what's outdated, find what's incomplete.
questions: When was this document last updated?
Do you have a digital copy? Can you get one from your lawyer?
Does your executor / POA person know they're named? Do they understand what that means?
Are the named people still the right people? Has anything changed?
Is the HIPAA release embedded in your healthcare proxy, or separate?
Do you have a DNR as a standalone document (not just referenced in the living will)?
Have you reviewed beneficiary designations recently?
priorityOrder: Get digital copies of all documents from the lawyer
Re-read the documents — don't assume you know what they say
Call executor / POA to confirm they know they're named and what to do
Identify the specific gap (standalone DNR, trust, updated beneficiaries, HIPAA)
Address the gap with the same lawyer or a new one

actionItems.text: Call your lawyer.
actionItems.script: Can you email me the digital versions of my will, POA, and medical directive?

actionItems.text: Read each document. Note anything that surprises you or that you want to change.
Call your executor / POA person.
actionItems.script: I want to make sure you know you're named and understand the responsibilities.

actionItems.text: Check the beneficiary on each account — especially after any life change.
Identify the gap document and schedule time to address it.

situationProfiles.quote: Matthew Perry had a trust, but he left one bank account out of the trust — it had $1.1M. Because he left it out, the trust had to go through probate. The old girlfriend was the beneficiary, not the new one. He never updated.

situationProfiles.name: Digital access gaps
questions: If you died tomorrow and your phone went with you, is there a Legacy Contact set up on your account so someone could actually get in?
Have you named someone to handle your social media if you couldn't do it yourself -- and do they actually know what to do?
Are your passwords and logins somewhere a trusted person could actually find and use?
priorityOrder: Legacy Contact on phone/device accounts -- the gateway to everything else digital
Named social media executor with real instructions, not just a name
Password manager or written access list a trusted person can actually use

actionItems.text: Set up a Legacy Contact on your phone account, so someone you trust could actually get in if something happened to you.
Name someone to handle your social media if you couldn't -- and write down exactly what you want done (memorialize, delete, or keep active).
actionItems.script: If something happened to me, here's what I'd want you to do with my accounts -- can I walk you through it?

actionItems.text: Put your passwords in a password manager, and make sure the person who'd need access knows how to get in.

situationProfiles.name: Physical (health and care) gaps
situationProfiles.urgency: parallel -- always additive, like profile_business_owner and profile_digital_gap
questions: Do you have a healthcare proxy or medical power of attorney -- someone who can make medical decisions for you if you can't?
Do you have an advance directive or living will that spells out your care wishes, like a DNR?
If you were in a serious accident tomorrow, does your healthcare proxy actually know your wishes in detail?
priorityOrder: Name a healthcare proxy -- the gateway to everything else in this domain
Put care wishes in writing with an advance directive
Actually walk your proxy through your wishes in detail, not just confirm they're named

actionItems.text: Name a healthcare proxy -- someone who can make medical decisions for you if you're unable to.
Put your care wishes in writing with an advance directive, so your proxy isn't guessing.
Sit down with your healthcare proxy and actually walk them through your wishes -- not just that they're the one making the call.
actionItems.script: If I couldn't speak for myself, here's what I'd want -- can I walk you through it?

quantityCap.rule: Maximum 5 action items per sprint/session. More kills momentum.
quantityCap.quote: I can give you a checklist and you'd be like, but we don't get it done for multiple reasons.
quantityCap.checkPrevious: Always ask first: 'What did you complete from last time?' before adding new items.

calendarAnchoring.rule: Every action item is tied to the member's real calendar, not an arbitrary deadline.
examples: Before you go to Florida (4-day urgency window)
This month — no rock climbing, no founder institute (leverage an open calendar)

noJudgmentFraming.rule: When items aren't completed, don't shame — normalize.
noJudgmentFraming.quote: Life gets busy. It's not prioritized. That's why it takes time. There's no judgment.

sequencingRule.rule: People → Documents → Access, in that order, every time.
steps: Identify the PEOPLE (who is the executor, POA, guardian, healthcare proxy)
Create the DOCUMENTS that formalize those roles
Ensure ACCESS (crucial doc box, digital vault, account access instructions)

conversationScripts.note: Niki gives exact language for difficult conversations. Reuse these verbatim where the profile matches — don't paraphrase, the phrasing is deliberately low-friction/non-confrontational.

scripts.context: Approaching an aging parent for documents
scripts.script: Mom, I'm doing my estate planning. I realized I don't have a copy of yours. If something happened to you tomorrow, I would be responsible. Can you give me a copy of your will, power of attorney, and medical advance directive?

scripts.context: Calling a financial institution about TOD/POD
scripts.script: I'm calling to say I'm doing estate planning. I just want to make sure: do I have transfer on death set up on this account? If not, how do we set that up?
If you don't need your credit available right now — not buying a car, not buying a house — your credit should be frozen. It's the best protection against the data breaches happening right now.

voiceAndGlossary.source: Elisa Seeherman meet-and-greet transcript — tone/vocabulary reference only, not an action-item source (this was a business-development conversation, not a coaching session).

terms.note: The deliverable/gift a member builds — something to hand to loved ones.
Replaces 'sandwich generation' — squished between caring for kids and aging parents.
Die tomorrow unexpectedly / terminal diagnosis / long-term care plan.

voiceAndGlossary.illustrativeStory: Elisa's father recorded a video interview with his mother-in-law and posted it to YouTube; YouTube's settings don't allow official downloads, and the original digital file's location is unknown. If the YouTube account or platform goes away, the family loses the video permanently — a concrete example of why digital-asset planning (not just legal/financial) matters.

definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A legal document that names your executor and states who receives your assets after you die.
A legal document that lets a trusted person make financial decisions on your behalf while you're alive but unable to make them yourself.

definitionsGlossary.term: Power of attorney (healthcare) / medical advance directive
definitionsGlossary.shortDefinition: A legal document naming who can make medical decisions for you if you can't, and stating your care wishes in detail.
A medical order stating you do not want CPR performed if your heart stops or you stop breathing.
The person legally responsible for carrying out your will after you die — settling accounts, distributing assets, closing the estate.
A designation on a brokerage or investment account that names who receives it directly when you die, skipping probate.
Like transfer-on-death, but for bank accounts — names who receives the account balance directly when you die.
The person or people named on an account (401k, life insurance, IRA) to receive it after you die. These override what your will says, and don't update automatically.
The court process of settling an estate when accounts or property don't have a named beneficiary or designation — slower and more public than a direct transfer.
Your online accounts, files, and business logins — who has access, and what happens to them, if you're gone.
The person legally named to raise your minor children if you (and your partner) are no longer able to.

generalResources.summary: A will is a legal document that names your executor and states who receives your assets after you die. Most people work with an estate attorney to draft one, though simple wills can sometimes be done through state-approved templates. Key steps: decide on your executor, list your major assets and who should receive them, name a guardian for minor children if applicable, then sign it according to your state's witnessing requirements (usually two witnesses, sometimes notarized).

Look for an attorney who specializes in estate planning or probate law specifically, not a general practice lawyer. Good starting points: ask friends/family for referrals, check your state or local bar association's lawyer referral service, or search your state bar's attorney directory. Many estate attorneys offer a free or low-cost initial consultation -- use it to ask about their flat-fee vs. hourly pricing before committing.

Probate is the court process of validating a will and settling an estate when assets don't have a named beneficiary or automatic transfer designation. It can take months, involves court fees, and becomes part of the public record. Assets with a named beneficiary (like TOD/POD-designated accounts, or life insurance) typically skip probate entirely and transfer directly -- which is why keeping those designations current matters.

An executor is legally responsible for carrying out a will: filing it with the probate court, notifying beneficiaries and creditors, paying the estate's debts and taxes, and distributing remaining assets. It's a real time commitment (often months), which is why it's worth discussing the role with someone before naming them, not just picking a name.

At a high level: decide burial vs. cremation, choose or confirm a funeral home, decide on a service type (religious, secular, memorial-only, etc.), and set a budget -- funeral costs vary widely by region and choices made. Pre-planning (even informally, in writing) removes a huge amount of decision-making pressure from family during an already hard time.
